checkpoint_context.active_numbering_note: Current checkpoint law advances AGENT_SAFETY_POLICY-0 as Σ-5 after the MCP interface model even though older planning artifacts may still retain stale Σ numbering or titles.

safety_policy_definition.summary: Agent safety in Dominium is the policy layer that decides whether a proposed action is safe, validation-gated, review-gated, strongly gated, or prohibited by evaluating authority, ownership, semantic, runtime, release, ambiguity, and auditability constraints after task classification and MCP exposure have already been resolved.

action_classes.description: Safe to perform within explicit scope without extra human review beyond the normal task request, provided no higher-risk dimension is triggered.
Permitted only if the validation floor is run and the action remains within explicit scope, authority, and auditability constraints.
May proceed only with explicit human review or approval because the action can alter important canonical, runtime, or policy surfaces.
Requires explicit human control, privileged review posture, or later doctrine before execution and is not part of default autonomous or routine tool-mediated scope.
Must be refused, deferred, or kept non-automated at the current doctrine level.

safety_dimensions.description: Risk that the action alters or contradicts canonical semantic law, capability meaning, representation law, or formalization law.
Risk that the action touches ownership-sensitive roots, unresolved ownership splits, or canonical-versus-derived authority boundaries.
Risk that the action flattens kernel, component, service, binding, state, or lifecycle distinctions or traps authoritative state.
Risk that the action affects release channels, trust roots, signing, publication, archive identity, updater behavior, or external commitments.
Risk associated with how many subsystems, roots, or authority layers the action can affect.
Risk that the action is difficult to reverse, hard to audit, or likely to outlive a normal local rollback.
Risk caused by ambiguous prompts, targets, authority sources, or expected outcomes.
Risk that projected, mirrored, cached, generated, or transitional artifacts are mistaken for canonical authority.
Risk that the action touches bridge-mediated domain interaction or creates hidden cross-domain coupling.
Risk that the action cannot be validated, traced, reviewed, or explained after the fact.

review_escalation_categories.description: Proceed only after the validation floor is satisfied.
Pause for explicit human review or approval before mutation.
Keep ownership-sensitive or unresolved roots quarantined rather than auto-resolving them.
Convert the request into planning, analysis, proposal, or evidence output rather than direct execution.
Treat the action as strongly gated and keep it outside routine autonomous or interface-driven execution.
Refuse current execution and defer until later doctrine, explicit review, or infrastructure exists.

validation_expectation_categories.description: Verify that the required target artifacts actually exist.
Confirm that the authoritative inputs used are the correct governing sources.
Parse touched JSON, schema, or other machine-readable artifacts where relevant.
Check consistency between normative and machine-readable outputs when both are produced.
Run git diff integrity checks such as git diff --check.
State what was intentionally not changed.
Report what validations were run and what was not run.

mcp_exposure_class_default_safety.notes: Read-only exposure can remain bounded when no higher-risk dimension is triggered.
Planning and proposal surfaces remain safe only when their outputs stay bounded and auditable.
Evidence and audit surfaces require validation discipline and must not promote evidence into canon.
Packaging stays distinct from release distribution and must remain support-artifact scoped.
Controlled mutation is not routine autonomy and requires explicit review posture.
Review-gated exposure never becomes routine allowed automation.
Non-exposed surfaces remain outside current execution scope.

default_task_family_classifications.notes: Planning and checkpoint work remains safe only when it does not silently change authority or sequence law.
Doctrine work may be lawful with review, but canon or foundational meaning changes escalate further.
Governance mutation is review-gated even when bounded.
Bounded semantic work may proceed only with review and escalates immediately when ownership or bridge law moves.
Runtime work can be implemented, but not as review-free authority.
Shell work can remain validation-gated when bounded, but it escalates when it begins to reshape runtime or governance meaning.
Release, trust, and publication work is never a routine autonomous class.
Audit work is a strong automation candidate, but its outputs do not become authority by themselves.
Structural convergence remains a privileged class, not default automation.
Packaging remains distinct from release and is safe only while that distinction holds.
Explanatory work is the safest mutation class when it does not alter canonical meaning.
Inspection remains safe while it stays read-only and scoped.

classification_floor_rules.notes: Foundational canon rewrites are not routine review-gated tasks.
Ambiguous ownership areas escalate rather than auto-resolve.
Bridge-mediated domain interaction remains highly sensitive.
State and continuity law may not be changed as routine runtime maintenance.
Release and trust work remains strongly gated.
Late live-ops classes wait for later doctrine.
If effects cannot be traced, mutation is not safe.
Low-risk read and explanation work can remain bounded.
Evidence and support-artifact work remains safe with validation discipline.

non_exposable_or_prohibited_categories.description: Canonical law may not be rewritten silently or by convenience.
Ownership review may not be bypassed or silently collapsed.
An actor may not choose the canonical root in an ambiguous split by convenience.
Publication, trust-root, signing, or release-channel changes are not normal autonomous action classes.
Live-ops, canary, rollback, distributed handoff, or similar actions remain out of scope without later doctrine.
Destructive broad refactors remain prohibited without explicit human control.
Actions whose safety depends on infrastructure or guarantees not yet formalized remain out of scope.
Runtime convenience may not override bridge, state, or lifecycle law.
Projected, mirrored, cached, or generated artifacts may not be promoted to truth or authority by convenience.

ownership_caution_flags.summary: fields/ remains canonical semantic field substrate and field/ remains transitional.
schema/ remains canonical contract law and schemas/ remains validator or projection facing.
packs/ and data/packs/ remain scoped ownership surfaces and are not interchangeable.
Canonical artifacts outrank projected, mirrored, cached, and generated evidence.
A runtime root name does not establish canonical runtime or safety authority.
Older planning numbering or order drift does not override the active checkpoint.

runtime_state_lifecycle_caution_flags.summary: Safety policy must preserve kernel, component, service, binding, state, and lifecycle distinctions.
No action may let service convenience redefine semantic ownership.
Runtime availability or suspension does not define truth existence.
Authoritative truth may not be trapped in transient, projected, or product-local state.
Lifecycle transitions may not silently alter ownership, truth, or continuity guarantees.
Runtime actions remain subordinate to cross-domain bridge law and domain-service binding law.

release_publication_trust_caution_flags.summary: Publishing or advancing release channels is not a routine autonomous action.
Trust roots, signing posture, and signing material changes remain strongly gated.
Support bundles and checkpoint packaging do not imply release distribution semantics.
Archive, release-manifest, and release-identity surfaces remain trust-bearing and review-heavy.
Work that affects public commitments, updater behavior, or trust-facing release policy is privileged.
